Why do you love your stack and how has it helped you?

Abdulkareem Mustopha - May 16
Items.Author: evansifyke

Items.Title: Javascript 变量 | Javascript Variable
Items.Description:
声明 Javascript 变量的 4 种方法：

  • 使用var
  • 使用let
  • 使用const
  • 什么是变量？

    变量是存储数据的容器

    例子：

     var x = 5; var y = 6; var z = x + y;

    何时使用 JavaScript var？

    始终使用var 、 let或const声明 Javascript 变量。
    从 1995 年到 2015 年，所有 JavaScript 代码都使用了var关键字。
    如果您希望代码在旧版浏览器中运行，则必须使用var 。

    何时使用 JavaScript 常量？

    如果您想要一般规则：始终使用const声明变量。
    如果您认为变量的值可以更改，请使用let 。
    在此示例中， price1 、 price2和total是变量：

     const price1 = 10; const price2 = 20; let total = price1 + price2; //30

    注意：两个变量price1和price2使用const关键字声明。 有恒定的值，不能改变。 变量total用let关键字声明。

    所有 JavaScript变量都必须用唯一的名称来标识。
    为变量构造名称的一般规则是：

    • 名称可以包含字母、数字、下划线和美元符号。
    • 名称必须以字母开头。
    • 名称也可以以 $ 和 _ 开头。
    • 名称区分大小写（a 和 A 不同的变量）。
    • 保留字不能用作名称。

    赋值运算符

    在 JavaScript 中，等号 ( = ) 是赋值运算符，而不是equal to运算符。

    声明一个 JavaScript 变量

    您可以使用var或let关键字声明 JavaScript 变量：

     var carName; or let carName;

    一个语句，多个变量

    let name='aji', lastName='dk',address:'Indonesia'

    值 = 未定义

    没有值声明的变量将具有未定义的值。



原文:

4 ways to Declare a Javascript Variable:

  • using var
  • using let
  • using const
  • using nothing

What are Variables?

Variable are container for storing data

example:

var x = 5;
var y = 6;
var z = x + y;

When to Use JavaScript var?

Always declare Javascript variables with var,let or const.
the var keyword is used in all JavaScript code from 1995 to 2015.
the let and const keywords were added to JavaScript in 2015.
if you want your code to run in older browser, you must use var.

When to Use JavaScript const?

if you want a general rules: always declare variables with const.
if you think the value of the variable can change, use let.
in this example, price1,price2, anda total, are variables:

const price1 = 10;
const price2 = 20;
let total = price1 + price2; //30

noted: the two variables price1 and price2 are declared with the const keyword. there are constant values and cannot changed. the variable total is declared with the let keyword.

JavaScript Identifiers

All JavaScript variables mus be identified with unique names.
The general rules for constructing names for variables are:

  • Names can contain letters, digits, underscores, and dollar signs.
  • Names must begin with a letters.
  • Names can also begin with $ and _.
  • names are case sensitive(a and A different variables).
  • reserved words cannot be used as names.

The Assignment operator

In JavaScript, the equal sign(=) is an assigment operator, not an equal to operator.

Declaring a JavaScript Variable

You can declare a JavaScript variable with the var or the let keyword:

var carName;
or
let carName;

One Statement, Many Variables 

let name='aji', lastName='dk',address:'Indonesia'

Value = undefined

A variable declared without a value will have the value undefined.
Items.Author: ajidk16

Items.Title: AWS 安全性非常复杂......或者非常简单 - 这就是您如何构建它！ | AWS Security is very complicated... or very simple - it's all how you architect it!
Items.Description:
由卡拉诺丁汉和曼努埃尔沃格尔撰写。

这篇博文是关于AWS 架构完善的框架系列的一部分； 它是什么，为什么这样做有意义，以及我们在 kreuzwerker 如何做到这一点。 在这篇文章中，我们将关注安全支柱——如何让安全成为你的第二天性。

它是什么——快速回顾

利用其架构师和客户的集体知识和经验，AWS 不断致力于构建架构完善的框架，其中包含有关如何在 AWS 云中架构和运行工作负载的关键概念、设计原则和最佳实践。 他们还想识别常见问题、决策和架构模式以及反模式。 换句话说，什么是架构良好的，什么不是架构良好的，并且让所有人都能获得这些知识，无论有人只是考虑迁移到云，还是已经在那里运行了数千个工作负载。

架构完善的框架建立在六大支柱之上

  1. 卓越运营👨🏽‍💻
  2. 安全🔒
  3. 可靠性💪🏾
  4. 性能效率🚀
  5. 成本优化💵
  6. 可持续性🌳

重要的是要注意架构完善的审查不是审计。 没什么好害怕的； 第一次没有把事情做好是不会被扣分的。 该过程通过几个基本问​​题和检查引导。 它源自与 AWS 云合作在安全性、成本效率和性能方面获得的多年经验。 因此，它为改进提供了很好的建议。 它可以帮助您为您的应用程序和工作负载构建安全、高性能、弹性和高效的基础架构。

关于 2022 年 AWS 架构完善的评论的确凿事实是：

  • 它包括所有支柱的 58 个问题
  • 一个工作量大约需要 4-6 小时（没有工具支持）
  • 目标是通过至少回答 20 个问题来纠正 45% 的高风险发现。

我们在这里更详细地从我们的角度描述这个过程。

我们如何在 kreuzwerker

你为什么要和我们一起做？

作为一个架构完善的合作伙伴，我们每年至少进行 20 次架构完善的审查，并为每个支柱和实践经验建立了全面的深厚架构专业知识。

我们如何进行这样的审查？

对我们来说，这是一个互动的过程：我们每次都会通过征求客户的反馈意见并进行简短的内部回顾来检查和调整。 截至目前，我们执行如下：

  • 我们从 09:00-12:00 和 13:00-15:00 分两个街区进行，午休时间。 但是，如果我们速度更快，我们可以不断适应，例如，我们改变差距，而且无论是远程还是在您的办公室，我们也很灵活。
  • 我们以互动、讲故事的方式进行。 这意味着：你说话，我们倾听，然后深入挖掘特定领域，同时能够涵盖多个问题。
  • 我们的流程得到了工具的支持（更多内容在博文系列的另一部分🥳）

我们不仅处理提问，而且指导回答问题。 我们可以告诉您如何以及为什么可以进行改进。 好了，介绍到此为止。 让我们直接进入它。

安全支柱

简而言之

无需定义对安全性的需求——我们都希望我们的数据和服务在需要时是完整的、可用的和保密的，我们希望我们的客户和员工能够按预期使用它们。 当我们考虑如何实现它时，事情变得有点模糊。

在 AWS 云中，我们应该首先定义您之间的责任线 - 云服务消费者 (CSC) 和 AWS - 云服务提供商 (CSP)。

进一步细分：AWS 将确保他们的基础设施免受不良行为者（以及 松鼠！）、火灾和洪水，或物理组件的常规“磨损”。 这是为了确保许多云消费者可以在相同的物理主机上运行他们的云服务并通过相同的物理网络电缆发送他们的数据 - 并且知道他们的工作负载是安全的并且与其他消费者的工作负载完全分离，因此是安全的。 继承这些控制使他们能够专注于他们共同承担的责任，并且在许多情况下，可以帮助 CSC 实现对各种网络安全框架的合规性。


图表显示了责任共担模型，来源：亚马逊网络服务

这就是 AWS 部分？ 作为云服务消费者，您负责“云中的安全性” ——这意味着其他一切。 从您的 AWS 账户本身开始，一直到您创建的每一个资源 - 您有责任确保其安全。 同时 - 让我们不要忘记可用性！ 安全性和可用性是天然的“敌人”——我们可以通过简单地拔掉网络电缆来轻松地“保护”我们的系统免受恶意外部人员的攻击——但这意味着合法用户无法使用它们。 那么我们该怎么做呢？ 是的，您猜对了 - 在AWS 架构完善的框架安全支柱的帮助下。

设计原则

实施强大的身份基础

首先确定将访问您的资源的身份，包括人类（用户、开发人员、合作伙伴）和机器（虚拟机、功能、应用程序）。

在许多情况下，使用集中式身份提供者来简化访问管理是有意义的。 您可以使用AWS Single Sign-On连接到外部身份提供商，或者如果您已经实施了它 - 使用您的Microsoft Active Directory 。

一旦您知道谁将访问您的资源，请确保正确的身份验证（登录过程）以使用密码策略，并尽可能强制使用多因素身份验证(MFA)。

继续询问谁以及如何访问您的数据和服务，确定角色和职责并将它们组合在一起。 例如，通过使用 AWS IAM 组和策略，您可以创建一组作为您的管理员的用户，然后通过仅允许他们从一组特定 IP 地址（您的办公室）连接时才允许他们执行操作来添加另一层安全性或您的 VPN 范围。 记住最小权限原则——执行功能所需的最小权限。 即使对他们来说有点不方便，如果合法用户缺乏完成工作的权限，他们也会来告诉你。 这种方式比过度配置和稍后听到实习生意外删除您的生产数据库要好。

启用可追溯性

监控、记录和审核您的环境中发生的事情。 它将使您能够发现问题并不断改善您的安全状况，从而降低您的业务风险。 或者——在最坏的情况下——这将有助于回答“谁做了什么，什么时候做的，为什么做的？” 这样当发生不好的事情时，您将能够判断这是真正的错误还是恶意攻击 - 并执行适当的纠正措施。

实施检测控制，以通知您环境中发生的意外事件。 在 kreuzwerker，我们是 AWS 安全和检测控制的热情倡导者，我们喜欢分享我们关于如何利用的知识：

  • 还有很多！ 在所有层应用安全性

    假设您的基础设施总是受到攻击。 那不是偏执狂。 这些天来，这是常识。 即使您没有被专门针对，请放心，某个地方的某个人正在发起“喷洒和祈祷”攻击 - 检查数百万个目标，以便轻松进行恶作剧。 如果其中一层被破坏，在所有层应用安全性将为您提供额外的保护。

    在实践中，这意味着您需要了解您的基础架构。 保持最新的资产和拓扑图 - 并实施信任边界、分段、配置、策略执行点等，以保护您的系统免受未经授权和无意的访问。

    对于网络保护，明确定义并区分面向公众的服务与内部服务。 您的公共访问点是您最容易受到攻击的一些点 - 确保正确保护所有路径和路由。AWS 提供了许多前向服务来为您提供额外的保护，其中包括Amazon Route53 （它不仅仅是托管您的域名，它是更多！）、 Amazon CloudFront 、弹性负载均衡器、 AWS WAF和AWS Shield 。 对于计算保护，首先确保通过将资源部署到已使用上述保护措施保护的网络来继承保护，并使用访问控制策略。 然后，您可以利用大量 AWS 解决方案来帮助您保护工作负载：AWS Systems Manager 允许您安全地登录控制台或在虚拟机上执行一次性命令和脚本，而无需外部 SSH 访问或堡垒主机。 它还可以帮助进行安全修补。 一旦确定了工作负载，最好通过删除操作系统预装的所有不必要的程序、脚本和驱动程序来减少攻击面。 然后，您可以将这些配置保存为您自己的 AMI （或使用来自 AWS 和 AWS 合作伙伴的可信 AMI）并使用您可以限制的策略，以便您的开发人员仅使用预先批准的 AMI。

    并且永远记得保护对您的 S3 存储桶的访问！ 如有疑问 - 使用IAM 访问分析器找出您的存储桶访问模式。

    自动化安全最佳实践

    您的架构将根据您的业务需求而增长和变化，因此请确保您的安全性跟上！

    在 AWS 中，您最好的朋友是AWS Config - 您可以使用一组AWS 定义或自定义规则来确保您的资源符合最佳实践。 根据您的设置，AWS Config 可以通知您不合规事件并且什么都不做，而且在 Lambda 函数的帮助下，它可以自动修复不合规的资源。 最后但同样重要的是 - AWS CloudFormation可以通过各种方式帮助您实现安全自动化。 将基础架构存储为代码有助于轻松进行审计和变更管理，并且在发生事故时，可以帮助您快速重新部署基础架构并恢复运营，从而减少停机时间和业务损失。

    保护传输中和静态数据

    最重要的是 - 了解您的数据。 为避免合规和法律问题以及增加的成本 - 仅存储您必须存储的数据，并且仅在必要时存储。

    实施数据分类程序和技术，根据敏感性、工作量、合规性、保留率分离数据。 开发标记模式将有助于对 S3 数据进行分类、管理和访问。 使用标记化和加密来保护敏感数据。

    静态数据正是它听起来的样子——静态数据持久化到存储中。 除了通过我们已经提到的适当控制来保护对您数据的访问之外，可能还需要对其进行加密。 您可以选择在将其提交到存储之前对其进行加密（客户端加密），或者您可以让 AWS 帮助您，使用S3 存储桶加密、 AWS 密钥管理系统 (KMS)或者如果您在严格监管的环境中运行 - CloudHSM 。

    传输中的数据从一个位置移动到另一个位置——通常是通过不安全的公共网络。 为了确保它不被泄露，它必须被加密。 使用 SSL/TLS 加密您的流量，让AWS Certificate Manager减轻管理、购买、颁发和部署公有和私有证书的负担。

    让人们远离数据

    虽然我们花了很多时间思考“陌生人的危险”，但许多安全漏洞并非源于恶意行为，而是出于善意、忠诚的员工的简单人为错误，他们只是按下了错误的按钮。 通过自动化您的流程，您可以降低来自人机交互的风险。

    为安全事件做准备

    即使采用了最佳安全实践，事件也会发生，因此请提前计划。 想想所有可能发生的坏事，以及你将如何应对。 将所有这些行动纳入响应计划。 确保每个人都知道他们在安全事件中的角色——在哪里可以找到文档、需要通知谁、需要采取什么行动以及以什么顺序； 需要封锁哪些资源，以及它将对您的生产环境产生什么影响。 这些不是您希望人们在正在进行的安全事件的混乱中提出的问题。 计划、教育、模拟、学习、记录发现、 开发运行手册- 并重复。

    使用AWS Fault Injection Simulator在受控环境中创建破坏性事件，并在发生真正的安全事件时创建补救措施。

    结论

    既然我们已经讨论了安全设计和解决方案，您可能会好奇我们在 kreuzwerker 是如何做安全的？

    首先，我们确保每个人都在船上。 我们最不想要的就是制定一个没有人知道、理解或关心的出色的安全计划。 这是最重要的部分——我们的安全实践必须帮助我们开展业务，而不是阻碍我们。 我们花时间向我们所有的同事（不仅是直接参与 IT 实践的人）解释我们的安全实践如何帮助保护我们，直到我们都理解并同意遵守这些实践的重要性。

    然后我们不断地重新评估我们的政策。 我们成长。 作为企业和个人，随着时代和环境的变化，新的威胁不断出现，我们的安全必须跟上！ 安全不是目标。 这是一个过程，我们总是这样对待它。

    我们也知道人们会犯错误，如果人们害怕受到惩罚，他们往往会隐藏自己的错误。 我们营造一个无可指责的环境，如果同事犯了错误，我们鼓励他们挺身而出，以便我们都能从中吸取教训并改正。 诚实是我们的基因，因为没有信任就没有安全！

    最后，我们永远不会停止学习和分享我们学到的东西。 我们相互通报新技术、威胁和事件。 我们在内部讨论它们，并在适当的时候通知我们的客户。 如果我们在其他人的代码中发现错误，我们一定要让他们知道。 同样 - 我们欢迎改进我们的解决方案。 我们认为安全是每个人的责任，我们尽自己的一份力量。



原文:

Written by Kara Nottingham and Manuel Vogel.

This blog post is part of a series about the AWS Well-Architected Framework; what it is, why it makes sense to do it, and how we at kreuzwerker do it. In this entry, we will focus on the Security Pillar - how to make security your second nature.

What it is - a quick recap

Using the collective knowledge and experience of their architects and clients, AWS is continuously working on a Well-Architected Framework, which consists of key concepts, design principles and best practices on how to architect and run workloads in the AWS Cloud. AWS developed Well-Architected Framework in order to understand what makes some customers succeed in the cloud while others fail. They also wanted to identify common problems, decisional and architectural patterns as well as anti-patterns. In other words, what is well-architected and what is not, and to make this knowledge available to all, regardless of whether someone is only just considering migrating to the cloud, or are already running thousands of workloads there.

Well-Architected Framework is built on six pillars

  1. operational excellence 👨🏽‍💻
  2. security 🔒
  3. reliability 💪🏾
  4. performance efficiency 🚀
  5. cost optimization 💵
  6. sustainability 🌳

The AWS Well-Architected Review process provides a consistent approach for customers and partners to evaluate architectures and implement scalable designs.

It's important to note that the Well-Architected Review is not an audit. It's nothing to be afraid of; there are no penalty points for not getting things right the first time. Well-Architected Review is a way of working together on how to improve your architecture. The process leads through several foundational questions and checks. It has been derived from years of experience that have been gained from working with the AWS cloud regarding security, cost efficiency, and performance. Hence, it provides good advice on improvements. It helps you to build secure, high-performing, resilient, and efficient infrastructure for your applications and workloads.

The hard facts about AWS Well-Architected reviews in 2022 are:

  • it consists of 58 questions in total across all pillars
  • it takes around 4-6 hours for one workload (without tool support)
  • the goal is to remediate 45% of the high-risk findings with a minimum of 20 questions answered.

We describe the process from our perspective in more detail here.

How we do it at kreuzwerker

Why should you do it with us?

As a Well-Architected partner, we do at least 20 well-architected reviews per year and have built overall deep architectural expertise for every pillar and hands-on experience.

How do we perform such a review?

For us, it's an interactive process: we inspect and adapt every time we do it by requesting feedback from our clients and doing a short internal retrospective. As of now, we perform it as follows:

  • We do it in 2 blocks from 09:00-12:00 and 13:00-15:00 with a lunch break. However, we can continually adapt if we are faster, e.g., we shift the gap, and we are also flexible whether doing it remotely or at your office.
  • We do it in an interactive, story-telling mode. This means: you talk, we listen, and then dig deeper into specific areas while being able to cover multiple questions.
  • Our process is supported by tools (more in the other part of the blog post series 🥳)

We do not just handle the questioning but give guidance to answering them. We can tell you how and why there could be improvements to be made. Alright, enough for the introduction. Let's jump right into it.

Security Pillar

In a nutshell

The need for security doesn't need defining - we all want our data and services to be complete, available and confidential when needed, and we want our clients and employees to use them as intended. Things get a bit murky when we think about how to achieve it.

In the AWS Cloud, we should start with defining the lines of responsibility between you - the Cloud Service Consumer (CSC), and AWS - the Cloud Service Provider (CSP).

AWS is responsible for "Security of the Cloud" - the facilities, networking, hardware and software that runs the AWS Cloud Services. AWS is always responsible for the physical and environmental security of their data center and what's in it, as well as the virtualization layer that provides the logical segmentation and isolation of services. To break it down a bit further: AWS will ensure that their infrastructure is protected from bad actors (and from squirrels, too!), fires and floods, or from regular "wear-and-tear" of physical components. AWS will also ensure that their infrastructure is up to date, patched and configured, and that their employees are trained and knowledgeable in security.

This is to assure that many cloud consumers can run their cloud services on the same physical hosts and send their data through the same physical networking cables - and be safe in the knowledge that their workloads are secure and entirely separated from those of other consumers. Inheriting those controls allows them to focus solely on their part of shared responsibility, and in many cases, can help CSCs achieve compliance with various cybersecurity frameworks.


Diagram shows The Shared Responsibility Model, source: Amazon Web Services

So that's the AWS part? As the Cloud Service Consumer, you are responsible for the "Security in the Cloud" - which means everything else. Starting with your AWS account itself and down to every single resource you create - it is your responsibility to make sure it's secure. At the same time - let's not forget availability! Security and availability are natural "frenemies" - we could easily "secure" our systems from malicious outsiders by simply unplugging the network cable - but that would mean rendering them unavailable for legitimate users. So how do we go about it? Yes, you guessed it - with the help of the AWS Well-Architected Framework Security Pillar.

Design principles

Implement a Strong Identity Foundation

Start with identifying identities that will access your resources, both human (users, developers, partners) and machine (virtual machines, functions, applications).

In many cases it makes sense to use a centralized identity provider to simplify access management. You can use AWS Single Sign-On, connect to an external identity provider or if you already have it implemented - utilize your Microsoft Active Directory.

Once you know who is going to be accessing your resources, ensure proper authentication - the logging in process - to make use of password policies and wherever possible, enforce the use of multifactor authentication (MFA).

Proceed by asking who and how should access your data and services, identify roles and responsibilities and group them together. For example, by making use of AWS IAM Groups and Policies you can create a group of users who are your Administrators and then add another layer of security by only allowing them to perform actions if they connect from a particular set of IP addresses - your office or your VPN ranges. (This is just a small example - AWS IAM Policies offer incredible levels of granularity!)

Remember the least privilege principle - the minimum permissions required to perform the function. Even if a bit inconvenient for them, if a legitimate user lacks the privileges to do their job, they will come and tell you. It's better this way around than over-provisioning and hearing later that an intern accidentally deleted your production database.

Enable Traceability

Monitor, log and audit what's happening in your environment. It will allow you to spot issues and continually improve your security posture, reducing the risk to your business. Or - in the worst case scenario - it will help answer "who did what, when and why?" so that when something bad happens, you will be able to tell if it was a genuine mistake or malicious attack - and perform appropriate corrective actions.

Implement detective controls that will notify and inform you about unexpected occurrences in your environment. AWS detective controls can learn about your environment and detect any deviations from regular patterns.

At kreuzwerker, we are passionate advocates of AWS security and detective controls and we love sharing our knowledge about how you can utilize:

  • AWS Security Hub - to centrally view and manage your security and compliance posture
  • Amazon GuardDuty - to monitor and detect threats
  • Amazon Inspector - to improve security and compliance posture of your applications running on virtual machines
  • AWS CloudTrail - to audit all event happening in your AWS environment
  • AWS Config - to build your resources and configuration inventory and ensure compliance
  • VPC Flow Logs - to help you understand your network security and flows by analysing traffic

and many more! - reach out to us about our Security Workshop to learn more!

Apply Security at All Layers

Assume your infrastructure is always under attack. That's not paranoia. These days that's common sense. Even if you're not being specifically targeted, rest assured that someone somewhere out there is launching a "spray-and-pray" attack - checking millions of targets for an easy way in to do mischief. Applying security at all layers will give you extra protection if one of the layers is breached.

In practice this means you need to know your infrastructure. Keep up to date diagrams of your assets and topologies - and implement trust boundaries, segmentation, configuration, policy enforcement points etc. that will protect your systems from unauthorized and unintentional access.

For network protection, clearly define and separate publicly facing services from internal services. Your public access points are some of your most vulnerable spots - make sure to properly secure all paths and routes in. AWS offers many forward facing services to offer you additional protection, amongst them Amazon Route53 (it's not just to host your domain names, it's a lot more!), Amazon CloudFront, Elastic Load Balancers, AWS WAF and AWS Shield. Virtual Private Networks (VPCs) within AWS can also be protected using a combination of solutions such as network access control lists (NACL), security groups, routing tables, NAT gateways or Egress-Only gateways for IPv6 networks.

For compute protection, firstly make sure you inherit protection by deploying your resources to networks that are already secured with aforementioned protections, and use access control policies. Then you can leverage a plethora of AWS solutions that will help you secure your workloads: AWS Systems Manager allows you to securely log into console or execute one off commands and scripts on your VMs without needing outside SSH access or bastion hosts. It can also help with security patching. Once you decide on a workload, it's a good idea to reduce attack surface by removing all unnecessary programs, scripts and drivers that may come pre-installed with the operating systems. You can then save those configurations as your own AMIs (or use Trusted AMIs from AWS and AWS Partners) and use policies that you can restrict so that your developers only use pre-approved AMIs.

And always remember to secure access to your S3 buckets! When in doubt - use IAM Access Analyzer to find out your buckets access patterns.

Automate Security Best Practices

Your architecture will grow and change in response to your business needs, so make sure your security keeps up!

In AWS, your best friend is AWS Config - you can use a sets of AWS defined or custom rules to ensure your resources comply with best practices. Depending on your setting, AWS Config can inform you about non-compliance event and do nothing, but also with the help of Lambda Function it can automatically remediate non-compliant resources. AWS Security Hub can continuously monitor your security posture and send findings to Amazon EventBridge (CloudWatch Events) for remediation.

And last but not least - AWS CloudFormation can help your security automation in various ways. Storing Infrastructure as code makes for easy auditing and change management, and in the event of an incident, can help you quickly redeploy your infrastructure and resume operations, reducing downtime and loss of business.

Protect Data in Transit and at Rest

Most importantly - know your data. To avoid compliance and legal headaches, as well as increased cost - only store data that you must store, and only as long as it's necessary.

Implement data classification procedures and techniques to separate data based on sensitivity, workload, compliance, retention. AWS Macie can help to automatically discover, classify and protect sensitive data stored in AWS. S3 lifecycle policies and Object Lock can automate retention and compliance. Developing tagging schema will help with categorizing, managing and accessing your S3 data. Use tokenization and encryption to protect sensitive data.

Data at rest is precisely what it sounds like - static data persisted to storage. Other than securing access to your data with proper controls we have already mentioned, it may be necessary to encrypt it as well. You can choose to encrypt it before committing it to storage (Client Side Encryption) or you can let AWS help you, using S3 bucket encryption, AWS Key Management System (KMS) or if you're operating in a heavily regulated environment - CloudHSM.

Data in transit moves from one location to another - often via insecure public networks. To ensure it's not compromised, it must be encrypted. Encrypt your traffic with SSL/TLS and let AWS Certificate Manager reduce the burden of managing, purchasing, issuing and deploying your public and private certificates.

Keep People Away from Data

While we spend a lot of time thinking about the "stranger danger", many security breaches do not originate as malicious actions, but a simple human error from well intentioned, loyal employee who just pressed the wrong button. By automating your processes you reduce the risk that comes from human interaction.

Prepare for Security Events

Even with the best security practices in place, incidents will happen, so plan ahead. Think about all the bad things that can happen and how you will respond. Put all those actions into Response Plan. Make sure everyone knows their role during the security events - where to find the documentation, who needs to be notified, what actions need to be taken and in what order; which resources need to be cordoned and what impact it will have on your production environment. Those are not the questions that you want people asking in the chaos of an ongoing security event. Plan, educate, simulate, learn, document findings, develop runbooks - and repeat.

Use AWS Fault Injection Simulator to create disruptive events in controlled environments and create remediation actions in case of a real security event.

Conclusion

So now that we have talked about security designs and solutions, you may be curious about how we do security at kreuzwerker?

Firstly, we make sure everybody is on board. The very last thing we want is to have a brilliant security plan that nobody knows, understands or cares about. This is the most important part - our security practice must help us do our business, not hinder us. We take time to explain to all our colleagues (not only those directly involved with IT practice) how our security practices help protect us, until we all understand and agree on the importance of adhering to them.

Then we continually re-evaluate our policies. We grow. As a business, and as individuals, and as times and circumstances change, new threats emerge and our security must keep up! Security is not a goal. It's a process, and we always treat it as such.

We also know that people make mistakes, and that people tend to hide their mistakes if they fear punishment. We cultivate a blameless environment and we encourage colleagues to come forward if they made a mistake, so that we can all learn from it and correct it. Honesty is in our DNA because there is no security without trust!

Lastly, we never stop learning and sharing what we learn. We inform each other about new technologies, threats, incidents. We discuss them internally and where appropriate, we notify our clients. If we spot a bug in somebody else's code, we make sure to let them know. And likewise - we welcome improvements to our solutions. We think security is everybody's responsibility and we do our bit.
Items.Author: kreuzwerker

Items.Title: 我的 Voi 踏板车在哪里：[1] 开始我的旅程 | Where's my Voi scooter: [1] Starting my journey
Items.Description:
我最近开始使用 Voi 滑板车，这是一项滑板车租赁服务，您可以使用停在街上的任何滑板车。 我通常把我的滑板车停在我家外面，这样我就可以开同一辆滑板车，第二天再出去。 然而，它总是被别人拿走。 虽然乘坐任何踏板车都很好，但我觉得不得不在其他地方找到踏板车很烦人。 因此，我决定编写一个程序来查找踏板车的去向。

查找 API（应用程序编程接口）

当您打开 Voi 应用程序时，它会向您显示每辆滑板车在地图上的位置。

因此，我决定找出是否有一个 API。 如果有的话，它会让我的工作更轻松。 我用谷歌搜索了“Voi API”，它把我带到了 Voi 的文档站点。 在那里，我发现了一个包含有关 MDS 和 GBFS V1,2 API 信息的质量灯。

搜索相关项目

那时，我决定停下来，看看有没有人做过类似的事情，所以我不会重新发明轮子（尽管那仍然是一个学习之旅）。 幸运的是，我找到了一篇文章，其中 Héctor Martos 分享了他如何追踪 Voi 踏板车的旅程。 他通过使用代理和 Voi 应用程序找到了 API，并且能够不断获取数据。 不幸的是，他没有说明 API 是什么以及如何使用它。

然后我做了一些进一步的研究，没有发现其他有用的信息，因此这就是我所拥有的。

回到 Voi 文档

回到大众之光。 显然，我需要使用不记名令牌对自己进行身份验证，以便查询他们的 MDS 数据。 但我不知道如何获得，我什至不知道 MDS 是什么，原来 MDS 是 Mobility Data Specification，由Open Mobility Foundation提供，它希望使用车辆的位置数据来帮助做出更好的决策。 我搜索了他们的网站，没有关于如何访问数据的信息。 所以我回到 Mass Light，我看到了这段代码片段来获取访问令牌：

 $ curl -X POST -u user:password -d grant_type = client_credentials -H "Content-Type:application/x-www-form-urlencoded" mds.voiapp.io/token

我做到了。 它不起作用，因为我需要一个用户名和密码，而我没有。 所以我认为这个 API 不适合普通用户。 如果他们从一开始就说明这一点，我会很有帮助。

找到一个关于使用 Voi API 的 Github 页面

我回去查找相关信息时，我偶然发现了这个Github 页面，其中有一个关于如何查询 Voi API 的教程。

所以我按照说明制作了一个python脚本：

 import requests url = "http://api.voiapp.io/v1/auth/verify/phone" # fake country code and phone number obj = { "country_code" : "AB" , "phone_number" : "0123123123" } re = requests . post ( url , json = obj ) print ( re . text ) print ( re )

但是，我得到了一个<Response [405]> ，直到后来我才发现我必须使用https而不是http ，我了解到的另一件事是英国的国家代码是GB 。
修复后，我能够得到响应和短信验证码，然后我按照Github 页面（存储库）上的步骤成功验证了自己。 我确实跳过了Get first authToken步骤，我认为这是因为我已经用我的电话号码在 Voi 注册了，我知道这一点是因为那天晚些时候当我尝试使用 Voi 应用程序时，我必须再次进行身份验证，所以我会有如果我仍然想用我的电话号码骑 Voi，请使用另一个号码继续这个项目。

找出区域代码

他们不可能告诉你每辆滑板车在哪里，因此我必须先获取我所在城市的区域代码，然后才能开始向 API 询问滑板车在哪里。

 url = "https://api.voiapp.io/v1/zones" re = requests . get ( url , headers = { "x-access-token" : accessToken }) print ( re . text ) print ( re )

这给了我一个巨大的 JSON（JavaScript 对象表示法）对象，但是借助control+f的强大功能，我很快找到了我所在城市的区域代码，并且可以开始查询踏板车的位置。

提出我的第一个车辆位置请求

然后我提出了我的第一个要求

url = f "https://api.voiapp.io/v2/rides/vehicles?zone_id= { my_zone_id } " re = requests . get ( url , headers = { "x-access-token" : accessToken }) print ( re . text ) print ( re )

它再次给了我一个巨大的 JSON 对象，格式为：

在这里，我们可以看到每辆滑板车都有一个 ID，以及经纬度的位置。 这里的滑板车似乎是停放的。

我将来打算做什么

有了这些数据，我打算找到一种方法来重复查询和记录车辆位置数据，以便随着时间的推移进一步分析它们。

我还计划看看是否可以像 Héctor Martos 的文章中那样跟踪踏板车的实时位置数据。



原文:

I got into using Voi scooter recently, it is a scooter hiring service where you can use any scooter parked on the street. I usually park my scooter outside my place so I can take the same scooter and go out again the next day. However, it keeps being taken by someone else. While it is completely fine to take any scooter, I find it annoying to have to find a scooter elsewhere. Therefore I decided to make a program to find where did the scooter go.

Finding an API (Application Programming Interface)

Voi scooters' locations are public. When you open the Voi app, it shows you where every single scooter is on the map.

Therefore I decided to find out if there is an API for that. If there is, it would make my job easier. I googled "Voi API", and it took me to a documentation site for Voi. In there I found a Mass light that contains information about an MDS and GBFS V1,2 API.

Searching for related projects

At that point, I decided to stop, and see if anyone has done anything similar before, so I don't reinvent the wheel (although that would still be a learning journey). Luckily, I found an article where Héctor Martos shared how he tracked a Voi scooter's journey. He found the API by using a proxy and the Voi app and was able to constantly fetch the data. Unfortunately, he didn't include what the API is and how to use it.

I then did some further research and found no other useful information, therefore this is all I had.

Going back to Voi documentation

Going back to Mass Light. Apparently, I need to authenticate myself with a bearer token in order to query their MDS data. But I didn't know how to get one, I didn't even know what MDS is, it turns out MDS is Mobility Data Specification, by Open Mobility Foundation, it hopes to use location data of vehicles to help make better decisions. I searched their website and there is no information on how to access the data. So I went back to Mass Light, I saw this code snippet to get an access token:

$ curl -X POST -u user:password -d grant_type=client_credentials -H "Content-Type:application/x-www-form-urlencoded"
  mds.voiapp.io/token

And I did. It didn't work because I need a username and password, which I don't have. So I figured this API is not for normal users. I would have been helpful if they stated this from the start.

Found a Github page on using the Voi API

I went back to find related information, and that is when I stumbled upon this Github page, with a tutorial on how to query the Voi API.

So I made a python script following the instruction:

import requests
url = "http://api.voiapp.io/v1/auth/verify/phone"
# fake country code and phone number
obj = {"country_code": "AB", "phone_number": "0123123123"}
re = requests.post(url, json=obj)
print(re.text)
print(re)

However, I got an <Response [405]>, and it is until later that I found out I had to use https instead of http, another thing that I learned is that the country code for UK is GB.
After fixing that, I was able to get a response and an SMS verification code, I then followed the steps on the Github page (repository) and successfully verified myself. I did skip the Get first authToken step, I think this is because I already registered with Voi with my phone number, I know this because later that day when I try to use the Voi App, I had to authenticate again, so I will have to use another number to continue this project if I still want to ride Voi with my phone number.

Finding out the zones code

Voi scooters serve 76 cites. It would be impossible that they tell you where every single scooter is, therefore I have to first get the zones code for my city before I can start asking the API where the scooters are.

url = "https://api.voiapp.io/v1/zones"
re = requests.get(url, headers={"x-access-token": accessToken})
print(re.text)
print(re)

This gave me a huge JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) object, but with the power of control+f, I quickly found the zones code for my city and can start querying the location of scooters.

Making my first vehicle location request

I then made my first request

url = f"https://api.voiapp.io/v2/rides/vehicles?zone_id={my_zone_id}"
re = requests.get(url, headers={"x-access-token": accessToken})
print(re.text)
print(re)

It again gave me a huge JSON object, with the format being:

Here we can see each scooter has an ID, and location in terms of longitude and latitude. The scooters here seem to be the ones parked.

What I plan to do in the future

With these data, I plan to find a way to repeatedly query and record the vehicle location data so that I can further analyse them over time.

I also plan to see if it is possible to track the live location data of the scooters, as done in Héctor Martos's article.
Items.Author: chit

Items.Title: 我的 Voi 踏板车在哪里：[2] 确定规格 | Where's my Voi scooter: [2] Deciding the specifications
Items.Description:
在之前的博客中我打下了基础，现在我知道如何查询 API，以及响应是什么。 在这篇博客中，我的目标是开始编写程序来获取滑板车数据。

当前问题：访问令牌生成

现在，每次我想查询位置时，我都需要提供一个访问令牌，该令牌来自使用我的真实令牌提交会话请求。 访问令牌将在 15 分钟后过期，因此我必须在那之后生成一个新令牌。

如果我计划频繁发送位置请求，比如说每分钟，我不想每次都发出会话请求和位置请求，因为它实际上使我发出的请求加倍，这是不礼貌的[1]。 因此，我希望找到一种仅在需要时获取访问令牌的方法。

我用这个词表示我希望最小化我发送到 API 服务器的请求

文献综述

我正在再次进行文献综述，因为我想确保我将要做的事情以前没有做过。 如果我先做这个项目，然后发现之前有人做了完全一样的事情，我会浪费所有的时间。 尽管有些人可能会争辩说我在做这件事时仍然会学到一些东西。

我再次搜索了有关 Voi 踏板车的其他项目。 我决定在 Github 上搜索，而不是在 Google 上搜索，因为我正在搜索代码。 令我惊讶的是，我实际上找到了相当数量的结果，这是我上次在 Google 上搜索时无法找到的。 结果包括：

上次我在谷歌上搜索这个，大部分结果都来自 Voi 网站，这次我决定使用-site:voiscooters.com以便排除来自该域的结果，这很有帮助。 这些是我在 Google 上找到的一些额外结果，可能会有所帮助：

回去工作

现在我已经了解了其他人在这个主题上做了什么。 是时候真正构建我的程序了。 我的计划是让程序不断查询 API 并保存结果，以供进一步分析。

我目前面临的挑战是：

  • 如何保存结果？ 我应该为每个查询创建一个文件吗？ 每分钟一个文件？ 每小时？
  • 如何保持结果简洁以节省存储空间？
  • 我应该什么时候申请访问令牌？
  • 但重要的一点是，我应该将我的令牌存储在一个单独的文件中，因为它是私有的，所以它不会提交给我的 git 历史记录。 否则，其他人将能够窃取我的身份并可能做坏事。 我从不小心提交了我的 Discord 机器人令牌时了解到这一点，幸运的是，Discord 在 Github 上运行了一个网络抓取机器人，它在坏人之前找到了我的令牌，它为我生成了一个新令牌并提醒了我这一点。

    从 git 历史记录中完全删除文件

    在研究这个主题时，我还发现了如何在 git 历史记录中完全删除一个文件，因为如果我提交我的令牌并在更新的提交中删除它，它仍然会在历史记录中。 完全删除它的方法是运行git filter-branch --index-filter "git rm -rf --cached --ignore-unmatch path_to_file" HEAD其中 path_to_file 是包含秘密的文件，然后git push -f to强制推送更改后的 git 历史记录。 副作用是它也会删除活动目录中的文件，因此请记住这一点。

    目前，我的计划是每分钟发出一个请求，即每天 1,440 个请求。 我还应该处理从 API 获得的数据，因为在上一篇博客中看到，响应包含很多无用的数据。 每个踏板车都有 id、short、电池、lng 和 lat 中的位置、zoneId、类别、锁定、lockType 和锁定状态。 但我需要的是：

    • 简短：因为它对应于 id
    • 电池：很高兴知道电池消耗
    • 位置：显然我正在尝试跟踪它
    • 锁定：我不确定滑板车是否已解锁，它是否仍会显示在系统上（编辑：已确认API仅返回锁定的滑板车，因此无需存储我将手动生成以下数据：
    • 车辆数量：查看返回了多少踏板车的位置
    • 时间戳：我什么时候提出这个请求的，为了更好的可读性，采用 ISO 格式

    我提出了一些请求，如果我只存储缩减的 JSON，缩进为 4：

     { "time_stamp" : "2022-06-01T08:14:42.305285" , "vehicle_count" : ---- , "vehicle_data" : [ { "short" : "----" , "battery" : -- , "lng" : ---------------------- , "lat" : ---------------------- , "locked" : true }, { "short" : "----" , "battery" : -- , "lng" : ---------------------- , "lat" : ---------------------- , "locked" : true },
...

    单个文件为 225KB，即 230,787 字节。 如果每天1440个这样的文件，那就是316MB，太多了，我会尽量减少这个。

    通过将数据存储为没有标签的列表：

     { "time_stamp" : "2022-06-01T08:20:09.340059" , "vehicle_count" : ---- , "data_format" : [ "short" , "battery" , "lng" , "lat" , "locked" ], "vehicle_data" : [ [ "----" , -- , ---------------------- , ---------------------- , true ], [ "----" , -- , ---------------------- , ---------------------- , true ],
...

    我将文件大小减小到 172KB，176,814 字节

    通过不使用indent=4 ：

     { "time_stamp" : "2022-06-01T08:21:39.708911" , "vehicle_count" : ---- , "data_format" : [ "short" , "battery" , "lng" , "lat" , "locked" ], "vehicle_data" : [[ "----" , -- , ---------...

    文件大小变成了 71.2KB，72,939 字节，我从没想过空间可以将文件大小推高这么多

    对于 1440 个请求，这将是每天 100MB 的数据，可能只有 66MB，因为它们只在早上 6 点到晚上 10 点运行，加上下雨时停止。

    我想我应该将记录分组为几个小时，所以我每天只生成 24 个文件，因为拥有一堆文件会降低操作系统的速度。

    我将来打算做什么

    现在我已经完成了所有必要的计算，我将开始编码，并在下一篇博客中看看它是如何进行的。



原文:

In the previous blog I laid down the groundwork, I now know how to query the API, and what is the response. In this blog, I aim to start writing the program to get the scooter data.

Current problem: access token generation

Now, each time I want to query the locations, I need to supply an access token, which comes from submitting a session request with my authentical token. The access token expires after 15 minutes, so I will have to generate a new one after that.

If I plan to frequently send location requests, say every minute, I won't want to make a session request and location request every time I do, as it effectively doubles the request I make, which is not polite[1]. Therefore I wish to find a way to only get an access token when I need it.

[1] The term polite comes from web scraping, which is to not send too many requests to a web server at the same time, to avoid overwhelming it. I am using this term to say that I wish to minimise the request I send to the API server

Literature review

I'm doing a literature review again because I want to make sure what I'm going to do have not been done before. If I did the project first and found out someone did something exactly the same before, I would have wasted all that time. Although some might argue I would still have learned something while doing it.

I searched again for other projects on Voi scooters. Instead of searching on Google, I decided to search on Github because I am searching for code. To my surprise, I actually found a decent amount of results, which I wasn't able to the last time I searched on Google. The results include:

The last time I searched on Google about this, most of the results are from the Voi website, this time I decided to use -site:voiscooters.com so that results from that domain would be excluded, and it helped. These are some extra results I found on Google that might help:

Back to work

Now that I've learned what others have done on this subject. It is time to actually build my program. My plan is to have the program constantly query the API and save the result, for further analysis.

The challenges that I currently face are:

  • How to save the result? should I create a file per query? a file per minute? per hour?
  • How do I keep the result concise to save storage space?
  • When should I request an access token?
  • Voi scooters are turned off at night, what happens then?

But one important thing is, that I should store my token in a separate file that is not committed to my git history since it is private. Otherwise, others would be able to steal my identity and potentially do something bad. I learned this from a time when I accidentally committed my Discord bot token, luckily, Discord runs a web scraping bot on Github and it found my token before the bad guys, it generated a new token for me and reminded me about that.

Remove a file completely from git history

While researching this topic, I also found out how to remove a file completely in the git history, because if I commit my token and remove it in a newer commit, it would still be in the history. The way to completely remove it is to run git filter-branch --index-filter "git rm -rf --cached --ignore-unmatch path_to_file" HEAD where path_to_file is the file that contains the secret, then git push -f to force push the altered git history. A side effect is that it would remove the file in the active directory too, so keep that in mind.

For now, my plan is to make a request every minute, which would be 1,440 requests per day. I also should process the data got from the API since as seen in the last blog, the response contains a lot of useless data. Each scooter has id, short, battery, location in lng and lat, zoneId, category, locked, lockType and lock status. But all I need is:

  • short: since it corresponds to the id
  • battery: would be nice to know the battery consumption
  • location: obviously I'm trying to track it
  • locked: I'm not sure if the scooter is unlocked, will it still show up on the system (edit: it is confirmed that the API only returns locked scooters, so no need to store And I will manually generate the following data:
  • vehicle count: see how many scooter's locations are returned
  • timestamp: when did I make this request, in ISO format for better readability

I made some requests, and if I just store reduced JSON, with an indent of 4:

{ "time_stamp": "2022-06-01T08:14:42.305285", "vehicle_count": ----, "vehicle_data": [ { "short": "----", "battery": --, "lng": ----------------------, "lat": ----------------------, "locked": true }, { "short": "----", "battery": --, "lng": ----------------------, "lat": ----------------------, "locked": true },
...

A single file is 225KB, which is 230,787 bytes. If 1440 such files a day, it will be 316MB, that's a lot, I will try to cut down on this.

By storing data as a list with no label:

{ "time_stamp": "2022-06-01T08:20:09.340059", "vehicle_count": ----, "data_format": [ "short", "battery", "lng", "lat", "locked" ], "vehicle_data": [ [ "----", --, ----------------------, ----------------------, true ], [ "----", --, ----------------------, ----------------------, true ],
...

I reduced the file size to 172KB, 176,814 bytes

by not using indent=4:

{"time_stamp": "2022-06-01T08:21:39.708911", "vehicle_count": ----, "data_format": ["short", "battery", "lng", "lat", "locked"], "vehicle_data": [["----", --, ---------...

the file size became 71.2KB, 72,939 bytes, I never thought some while space could drive up file size so much

that will be 100MB of data each day for 1440 requests, maybe only 66 MB since they only operate from 6 am to 10 pm, plus stop when it is raining.

I'm thinking I should group the records into hours, so I only generate 24 files a day, since having a bunch of files will slow down the operating system.

What I plan to do in the future

Now that I have done all the necessary calculations, I will start coding and see how it goes in the next blog.
Items.Author: chit

Items.Title: 在隧道模式下使用 Azure Bastion 将文件上传到 Azure 虚拟机 | Upload files to Azure Virtual Machines with Azure Bastion in tunnel mode
Items.Description:
在今天的教程中，我们将了解Azure Bastion中可用的一项很酷的新功能，我们可以使用本机客户端将文件上传和下载到 VM。 您可以在 Microsoft Docs 上阅读有关此功能的更多信息。

借助标准 SKU 提供的新 Azure Bastion 本机客户端支持，您现在可以：

  • 使用 Azure CLI 和本地计算机上的本机客户端通过 Azure Bastion 连接到目标 Azure 虚拟机。
  • 使用 Azure Active Directory 凭据登录到加入 Azure Active Directory 的虚拟机。
  • 访问您选择的本机客户端可用的功能（例如：文件传输）。

今天，我们将具体研究如何使用WinSCP从基于Windows 操作系统的本地计算机上传文件到使用隧道模式下的 Azure Bastion 的 Linux Azure VM。

在开始之前，我想先说明一些重要的注意事项和限制：

  • 仅使用本机客户端支持文件传输。 不能使用 PowerShell 或通过 Azure 门户上传或下载文件。
  • 要上传和下载文件，您必须使用Windows本机客户端和RDP 。
  • 您可以使用您选择的本机客户端和RDP或SSH将文件上传到 VM。
  • 此功能需要标准 SKU 。 基本 SKU 不支持使用本机客户端。

在此演示中，由于我们的目标 VM 运行的是 Linux 操作系统，因此我们将在隧道模式下使用 Azure Bastion，我们将只能上传文件，尚不支持下载文件。

如果您想了解如何使用本机 Windows RDP 客户端上传和下载文件，请参阅：上传和下载文件 - RDP

先决条件

要开始，您需要做一些事情，首先：

  • 要将文件上传到的 VM 的资源 ID。 设置 Azure Bastion（标准 SKU）

    注意：在设置 Azure Bastion 主机之前，我们需要一个带有/26子网的 Azure 虚拟网络，称为AzureBastionSubnet 。 我已经在我的环境中设置了 VNET 和子网：

    接下来，我将在名为Bastion_Setup.ps1的 PowerShell 脚本中使用Azure CLI来设置堡垒主机：

     #### Ensure VNET and AzureBastionSubnet with /26 CIDR is available before creation of Bastion Host ####
#Login to Azure
az login
az account set --subscription "Your-Subscription-Id" #Set Variables
$location = "uksouth"
$bastionName = "Pwd9000-EB-Bastion"
$bastionPip = "Pwd9000-EB-Bastion-Pip"
$bastionRG = "Pwd9000-EB-Network"
$bastionVNET = "UKS-EB-VNET" #Deploy Public IP for Bastion
az network public-ip create --resource-group $bastionRG ` --name $bastionPip ` --location $location ` --sku "Standard" #Deploy Bastion
az network bastion create --name $bastionName ` --public-ip-address $bastionPip ` --resource-group $bastionRG ` --vnet-name $bastionVNET ` --location $location ` --sku "Standard"

    该脚本创建了一个公共 IP 和堡垒主机，如下所示：

    接下来，我们将启用本机客户端支持。 如下所示导航到堡垒配置并启用本Native client support ：

    注意：如果您正在运行 Azure Bastion 的Basic SKU ，您还可以使用此区域将 SKU 升级到Standard 。 升级后，如果不删除并重新配置 Bastion，就无法恢复到基本 SKU。 目前，只能在 Azure 门户中配置此设置。

    打开堡垒隧道

    现在，我们的 Azure Bastion 设置和配置完成后，我们将打开一条通过 Azure Bastion 到我们的 Azure 托管 Linux VM 的安全隧道，然后我们可以使用 WinSCP 连接到该隧道，开始将文件上传到我们的 VM。

    导航到 Azure 门户中的 Linux VM，转到属性并记下资源 ID ，因为我们在打开堡垒隧道时将需要此值。

    接下来，打开 PowerShell 并使用您的环境变量运行以下Open_Tunnel.ps1脚本以在端口50022上打开隧道：

     #Login to Azure
az login
az account set --subscription "Your-Subscription-Id" #Set Variables
$bastionName = "Pwd9000-EB-Bastion"
$bastionRG = "Pwd9000-EB-Network"
$targetVmResourceId = "/subscriptions/829efd7e-aa80-4c0d-9c1c-7aa2557f8e07/resourceGroups/Linux-Vms/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/mylinuxvm9000" az network bastion tunnel --name $bastionName ` --resource-group $bastionRG ` --target-resource-id $targetVmResourceId ` --resource-port "22" ` --port "50022"

    如您所见，我们现在在本地 Windows 机器 (127.0.0.1) 上的端口50022上打开了一个隧道。

    注意：不要关闭这个 shell 窗口，因为它会关闭隧道，让会话保持打开状态。

    将 WinSCP 连接到正在运行的堡垒隧道

    接下来，我们将打开 WinSCP 并连接到我们的 localhost (127.0.0.1) 到正在运行的隧道上的开放端口： 50022 ：

    注意：文件协议是SCP ，用户名和密码是我们目标 VM 的。

    但是在我的情况下，我将使用私钥而不是密码进行连接。 为此，在上面屏幕上的 WinSCP 中单击高级并在 SSH -> 身份验证部分下选择要使用的私钥。

    注意：如果您使用PEM私钥，WinSCP 将自动创建PPK格式的PEM转换副本。 如果您在以下屏幕中选择“确定”，它将询问您将转换后的PPK格式密钥保存在哪里：

    现在使用 WinSCP 中配置的私钥，清除密码并选择Login 。 然后您将看到有关连接到未知服务器的警告，单击是继续：

    就是这样，您现在可以简单地将文件从左窗格（本地 Windows 机器）拖放到右窗格上的 Azure Hosted Linux VM 中：

    我们仍然可以使用 Azure 门户通过 Bastion Host 连接到我们的 VM 并检查我们上传的文件：

    我希望你喜欢这篇文章并学到了一些新东西。 您还可以在我发布的Github页面上找到这篇博文中使用的代码示例。 作者

    喜欢，分享，关注我：🐙 GitHub | 🐧 Twitter | 🐧推特| 👾 LinkedIn 👾领英



原文:

In todays tutorial we will take a look at a cool new feature that is available to us in Azure Bastion whereby we can upload and download files to a VM using a native client. You can read more about this feature on Microsoft Docs.

With the new Azure Bastion native client support, available with Standard SKU, you can now:

  • Connect to your target Azure virtual machine via Azure Bastion using Azure CLI and a native client on your local machine.
  • Log into Azure Active Directory-joined virtual machines using your Azure Active Directory credentials.
  • Access the features available with your chosen native client (ex: file transfer).

Today we will look specifically at how we can upload files from a Windows OS based local machine using WinSCP to a Linux Azure VM using Azure Bastion in Tunnel Mode.

There are a few important notes and limitations I would like to cover off first before we start:

  • File transfers are supported using the native client only. You can't upload or download files using PowerShell or via the Azure portal.
  • To both upload and download files, you must use the Windows native client and RDP.
  • You can upload files to a VM using the native client of your choice and either RDP or SSH.
  • This feature requires the Standard SKU. The Basic SKU doesn't support using the native client.

In this demonstration, since our target VM is running a Linux OS we will be using Azure Bastion in tunnel mode, we will only be able to upload files and downloading files is NOT supported yet.

If you want to see how you can Upload and Download files using the native Windows RDP client, please see: Upload and download files - RDP

Pre-requisites

To get started you'll need a few things, firstly:

  • An Azure Subscription
  • An Azure Bastion (Standard SKU)
  • Azure CLI (Version 2.32 or later)
  • WinSCP (Version 5.19.6 or later)
  • The Resource ID of the VM to which you want to upload files to. (In my case it will be a Linux VM hosted in Azure).

Setting up an Azure Bastion (Standard SKU)

Note: Before we can set up an Azure Bastion host we need an Azure Virtual Network with a /26 subnet called AzureBastionSubnet. I already have a VNET and subnet set up in my environment:

Next I will be using Azure CLI in a PowerShell script below called: Bastion_Setup.ps1 to set up the Bastion Host:

#### Ensure VNET and AzureBastionSubnet with /26 CIDR is available before creation of Bastion Host ####
#Login to Azure
az login
az account set --subscription "Your-Subscription-Id" #Set Variables
$location = "uksouth"
$bastionName = "Pwd9000-EB-Bastion"
$bastionPip = "Pwd9000-EB-Bastion-Pip"
$bastionRG = "Pwd9000-EB-Network"
$bastionVNET = "UKS-EB-VNET" #Deploy Public IP for Bastion
az network public-ip create --resource-group $bastionRG `
 --name $bastionPip `
 --location $location `
 --sku "Standard" #Deploy Bastion
az network bastion create --name $bastionName `
 --public-ip-address $bastionPip `
 --resource-group $bastionRG `
 --vnet-name $bastionVNET `
 --location $location `
 --sku "Standard"

The script created a Public IP and Bastion host as follow:

Next we will enable native client support. Navigate to the Bastion Configuration as shown below and enable Native client support:

Note: If you are running the Basic SKU of Azure Bastion, you can also use this area to upgrade the SKU to Standard. Once you upgrade, you can't revert back to the Basic SKU without deleting and reconfiguring Bastion. Currently, this setting can be configured in the Azure portal only.

Opening a Bastion Tunnel

Now with our Azure Bastion set up and configured we will open a secure tunnel through Azure Bastion to our Azure hosted Linux VM, which we can then connect to using WinSCP to start uploading files to our VM.

Navigate to the Linux VM in the Azure portal, go to Properties and make a note of the Resource ID as we will need this value when we open the Bastion tunnel.

Next, open PowerShell and run the below Open_Tunnel.ps1 script using your environments variables to open a tunnel on port 50022:

#Login to Azure
az login
az account set --subscription "Your-Subscription-Id" #Set Variables
$bastionName = "Pwd9000-EB-Bastion"
$bastionRG = "Pwd9000-EB-Network"
$targetVmResourceId = "/subscriptions/829efd7e-aa80-4c0d-9c1c-7aa2557f8e07/resourceGroups/Linux-Vms/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/mylinuxvm9000" az network bastion tunnel --name $bastionName `
 --resource-group $bastionRG `
 --target-resource-id $targetVmResourceId `
 --resource-port "22" `
 --port "50022"

As you can see we now have a tunnel open on port: 50022 on our local Windows machine (127.0.0.1).

Note: Do not close this shell window as it will close the tunnel, leave the session open.

Connect WinSCP to the running Bastion Tunnel

Next we will open WinSCP and connect to our localhost (127.0.0.1) to the open port: 50022 on the tunnel that is running:

Note: The file protocol is SCP and the UserName and Password is that of our target VM.

However in my case I will connect with a private key instead of a Password. To do so, in WinSCP on the screen above click on Advanced and select the private key you want to use under the SSH -> Authentication section.

Note: If you are using a PEM private key, WinSCP will automatically create a converted copy of the PEM in PPK format. If you select OK in the following screen it will ask you where to save the converted PPK formatted key:

Now with the private key configured in WinSCP, blank out the password and select Login. You will then see a warning about connecting to an unknown server, click Yes to continue:

And that is it, you can now simply drag and drop files from the left pane (local Windows machine) into the Azure Hosted Linux VM on the right pane:

We can also still use the Azure portal to connect to our VM via the Bastion Host and inspect the files we have uploaded:

I hope you have enjoyed this post and have learned something new. You can also find the code samples used in this blog post on my published Github page. ❤️

Author

Like, share, follow me on: 🐙 GitHub | 🐧 Twitter | 👾 LinkedIn
Items.Author: pwd9000

Items.Title: 测试 2.0 | Testing 2.0
Items.Description:
测试是多种多样的。 尽管主要目标在所有地方都是相同的，但项目之间存在许多差异：不同的堆栈、技术、流程和方法。 这个问题就是效率低下。 目前，测试是开发生命周期中优化程度最低的过程之一。

今天，我们将尝试了解它是如何发生的以及我们能做些什么。

这一切是从哪里开始的？

通常，在项目开始时没有测试人员，开发人员和暂定经理负责产品的质量和发现的错误。 在这个世界上一切都很好，因为如果经理发现了一个错误，他就会将它发送给开发人员进行更正； 开发者修复了这个bug，大家都很开心。 但是，向前发展，开发人员的数量会增加。 这意味着代码量增加了。 更多代码意味着更多错误！ 此刻，经理开始缺乏力量，但他努力应对，因为如果不是客户，还有谁应该测试系统？ 他知道一切应该如何工作，检查一切是否按照应有的方式完成，同时他也带来了错误。

出色的！ 当经理继续这样工作并最终错过一个严重错误时，就会出现问题。 此时，项目成员做出决定，经理（作为产品神圣知识的承载者）应该测试新功能，而不是将时间浪费在无聊的回归测试上。 所以我们得到了我们的第一个手动测试仪。 为什么要手动？ 因为开发人员在创建正确的回归自动化方面还没有任何专业知识，而且经理更容易和更冷静地信任人而不是代码。 现在我们有了一个团队，没有任何困难：经理测试新功能和特性，测试人员推动回归。 简单明了。 但是，当一个严重的错误潜入生产环境时，混乱就开始了。 团队将去找测试人员并询问：

你是怎么错过的？ 我们的专家将诚实回答
我肯定检查过这个地方！ 最有可能的是，开发人员在我测试后干预了代码！
显然，在回顾性分析了情况后，经理决定将测试形式化并在测试中引入检查表。 无论检查表是简单的还是复杂的，基于普通的专业软件还是 Excel，它们都将清楚地显示正在测试的内容、时间和结果。 使用此工具，团队将能够准确了解测试人员是否遗漏了错误或粗心的开发人员在冒烟后未测试就推出了功能。 这就是我们的生活方式。

产品不断增长，获取功能； 检查清单的数量不断增加； 我们的测试人员正在获得经验和权威，而他的测试时间已经不多了。

是时候让另一位测试人员支持他了！ 现在，我们的开发周期中出现了一组测试人员。 现在有两个人测试回归。

团队中出现了第一个Test Management System（以下简称TMS），因为现在除了标准的“step、status、time”字段外，我们还需要记录测试者头部的一堆数据：links、login ，密码，一些额外的步骤或绕过旧的已知小东西。 所以我们有测试用例允许我们：

  • 轻松招募新人
  • 不必太担心入职和转移产品知识
  • 在团队成员之间分发测试
  • 监控测试人员的工作表现和质量

但是，也有缺点：与检查表不同，测试用例需要不断更新和适当的组织。 如果更新是一个谨慎和过程的问题，那么测试用例的组织是一个复杂的问题。 当测试服务器或登台出现在测试基础设施的自然增长过程中时，就会出现问题。 至此，测试文档包括了运行所需的所有细节：链接、登录名和密码、缓存和 cookie 等。但是随着预生产测试环境的引入，所有这些细节都必须被锁定并参数化，因为它们在生产和测试服务器上不匹配。 然后测试团队将不得不投入两倍的精力来支持所有这些东西。

产品不断增长并获得更多功能； 测试用例的数量不断增长； 我们的测试员成为领导者。

团队也在不断壮大，更多的测试人员加入进来。但生产力并不是线性增长，而是趋于对数函数：每个新工程师对团队生产力的提升幅度较小。 有那么一刻，截止日期开始像雪崩一样增长，经理意识到团队需要……

自动化！


当第一位自动化工程师加入团队时，他们可能会使用团队没有专业知识的框架和方法。 这意味着团队被迫完全依赖新员工的经验和知识，如果在招聘阶段出现错误，很难发现，更不用说纠正了。

男人的肉是机器人的毒药！ 自动化测试不是人。 自动化测试是愚蠢的，他们不知道如何思考步骤并从场景中跳出来。 因此，将测试用例“翻译”成框架语言的经典方法是一种反模式，会导致错误的失败或遗漏错误。

想象一下，在 100 次测试中，您需要在其中登录、生成文档并以 PDF 格式下载它。 如果有任何细微的变化，人们会继续前进，并指出更新文档会很好。 如果是机器人，你会得到100个红色测试。

当测试人员看到 100 个红色测试时，他们会怎么做？ 没错，他们认为测试是错误的！

没有人想解决一百个相同的故障，所以让我们让自动化工程师来处理它们。 此时，团队走到了进化的死胡同：有一个自动化工程师自己写自动化测试，然后自己整理报告。 他本身就变成了一个东西，脱离了团队。

文档不会自己写

为了以某种方式控制测试自动化，QA 负责人决定将自动化和手动测试组合到一个流程中。 以下是有关如何执行此操作的三个选项：

  • 将测试自动化与 TMS 集成。 几乎所有的测试管理系统都有一个 API，以便团队可以将测试结果推送到系统中以获取汇总报告。 这里的问题很简单：自动化结果通常是在没有元数据和细节的情况下收集的，测试团队只能从报告中了解到 N 次自动测试失败。 为什么？ 您必须深入了解测试运行的内容并自己弄清楚。
  • 分开的自动测试运行，然后是手动回归。 这个选项尽管是“老派”，但在检测错误方面更有效。 这里的困难在于测试团队将不得不在两个界面中工作：在一个工具中处理自动化报告，而在另一个工具中手动运行。 假设测试人员创建一个运行并从他们的界面运行它； 之后，自动化程序运行他们的测试并向手动测试人员提供一份引诱报告。 如果报告中有错误，团队必须处理 Allure，然后他们也会尝试将检测到的错误转移到手动 TMS。 然后到 JIRA。 这是一个很大的努力。
  • 自动化成为一项完全独立的服务。 一旦测试自动化，它就会从手动测试注册表中删除。 如果您可以将自动化人员挑选到一个单独的部门并从他们那里获得“绿色管道证书”，为什么还要将手动测试与自动化联系起来呢？ 这可能是最危险的路径，因为它使一个单元完全依赖于另一个单元，如果在交叉路口出现问题，回想起来，这种联系往往会以冲突和混乱告终。

尽管困难重重，自动化工程师在一段时间后开始更有效率地工作：运行自动化测试不需要工程师的时间和注意力。 这意味着编写和运行的测试越多，与手动过程相比，我们在运行中节省的时间就越多。

当有十个自动化测试时，效果是不可理解的； 当有一百个时，它是显而易见的。 每天，工程师都会扩大覆盖范围，让自动测试在工作中变得越来越方便：更原子、更稳定、信息量更大。 这不正是我们所期望的吗？ 这一刻，可能会认为自动化已经到来，而且只会越来越好。 仿佛！

成长问题

一旦自动化测试开始增长，就会出现一堆新问题，而且这些问题更接近于开发而不是现有测试。 如果您的开发人员和测试人员没有建立密切的合作和经验分享，那么解决这些复杂性中的每一个都需要时间和精力。 那么我们是为了什么？

测试服务器稳定性

测试环境肯定会出现。 它将最终进入运营项目支持团队 (Ops) 管道，并且测试可能无法对其进行管理访问。

通常，一台非超级生产机器被用作测试服务器，当有更多的测试或运行时，它开始阻塞。 这通常会导致延迟交付运行结果和测试不稳定（在这种情况下，这意味着测试由于并行化中断或超时而失败），从而加剧了手动测试和自动化对自动化的信心已经薄弱的火上浇油。开发/运营。

自动化退出流程

由于这种进步，即使是最有能力的自动化人员也能完成自己的任务：

  • 手动测试积极与团队沟通，谈论测试结果和错误。 由于上述困难，自动化测试仍然是 QA 团队的内部工件，这并没有让自动化工程师有机会从生产团队“呼吸新鲜空气”。
  • 通常很难向开发团队添加任何带有元数据（例如 ID）的自动化测试，因为自动化工程师被认为在编码方面不如普通开发人员合格，因此他们被坚决解雇.
  • 能力上的差距也阻碍了进入运维团队； 结果，管理员驳回了提供的帮助，说“我们没有时间教你，我们会自己设置一切，你在家里使用它。” 或者“你想要一个 Selenoid 星团吗？ 慢点，我们的管道中没有码头工人！”

结果，我们遇到了这样一种情况，即工程师必须打破许多壁垒和孤岛来“合法化”自动化，而如果没有优秀经理的支持，这些事情就会失败。 最后，自动化者放弃了，他们只是在他们的测试环境中编写自动测试并显示漂亮的无用报告。 最后，自动化测试的问题多于答案：

  1. 我们不知道编写和运行了多少自动测试。
  2. 目前尚不清楚自动化测试涵盖了哪些功能，哪些没有。
  3. 不知道哪些测试相关，哪些不相关。
  4. 这些测试如何运行尚不清楚。
  5. 有必要找出存储测试结果的位置和方式。 最后，我们进行了手动测试，它处理回归而不是扩展。 旨在解决扩展问题的自动化团队被剥夺了权力，并且在没有同事支持的情况下，会滋生基础设施和流程复杂性。 测试变得无效。

什么是要做？ 测试 2.0

如果您有一个大型项目，并设定了计算手动测试每个版本的成本的目标，您将意识到自动化是必不可少的。 这就是为什么任何大型 IT 企业都围绕自动化构建测试的原因。

有人说“测试不是必需的——开发本身会编写测试，而金丝雀部署会检查其他一切”，但这并不完全正确。 这就是区别：

  • 开发人员 90% 的时间都在编写乐观测试。 检查幸福路径的测试：当您输入正确的数据时，您会得到正确的结果； 数据库准确存储和更新数据； 数据结构和算法正确执行。
  • 相反，测试人员使用破坏性范式进行测试。 他们的任务是在基本和意外情况下发现漏洞。 尽管一切通常都很好，但还是要找到它们。

让我们倒带一点时间来了解我们什么时候走错了路。 这是一位自动化工程师加入团队的时刻……不，决定本身是正确的！ 但是，让我们再次尝试考虑他应该被放在哪里，这样我们就不会陷入自动化脱离电网的情况。 以下文字在您看来可能是明显船长的故事，或者完全是胡说八道。 无论如何，请在 Twitter 上与我们联系！ 一篇文章不能一概而论，所以让我们用热烈的讨论来补充它。

自动化负责！

让我们翻转范式！ 现在几乎所有业务中对开发团队的信任都是惊人的（DevOps 的荣耀！）：

  • 开发人员具有评估代码及其测试方式的能力。
  • 发展有巨大的信任。
  • 开发与管理和运营团队有着密切的关系。

如果您仔细查看此列表，您可以指出每个项目都允许您修复测试自动化问题的相应来源。 让我们想象一下，如果 QA 自动化人员到达的那一刻，他不再是手动测试部门的齿轮，而是带领整个团队走向自动化。 从旅程的一开始，这将稍微改变他的责任和任务的整个向量。 在我们的雷达上至少有三个重大故事，当一个“权力的变化”以革命性的方式发生时，由于一个很酷的团队和一个关于管理层面自动化至高无上的意志坚定的决定。

第二种选择适用于没有超级合格的测试人员团队，开发人员自己负责测试的团队。 将自动化器交给开发团队的决定很明确。 这样的开发人员具有经验分享文化和足够高的测试文化，因此我们的工程师将有一些东西可以学习，并且有人可以展示代码以供审查。

  • 你不必担心能力。 这些开发人员将很乐意帮助自动测试人员找出我们在之前的场景中没有接触到的一系列新技术：Docker、git、CI/CD 管道。
  • 开发人员不喜欢编写自动化测试。 对他们来说，为一个特性编写测试是一件苦差事，只有拉取请求才能通过代码审查。 但这对自动化工程师来说是一项艰巨的任务。 他既可以处理该功能，又可以编写更有趣的测试。 试想一下，如果能腾出开发人员 20-25% 的时间来开发新功能，那该多好。
  • 开发团队也经常与 Ops 团队进行更好的沟通。 如果突然间测试开始疯狂失败，管理员将帮助您解决问题或分配更多 CPU/RAM。 为什么？ 因为如果开发中的测试会减慢发布速度，那么这对管道中的每个人来说都是一个问题。

当然，从长远来看，这将带来重大的组织变革。 我们将自动化置于测试的最前沿，而不是使其成为手动测试工具。 手动测试将成为自动化测试的工具！

你见过这样的事吗？ 但是想一想，我们真的需要手动测试人员来手动运行回归吗？ 他们应该创建新的场景并将它们传递给自动化，默认情况下，他们负责探索性测试和基础测试方面的专业知识，以确保最大的质量和覆盖率。 请让我知道这篇文章是否有见地，或者至少，读起来很愉快！ 如果是这样，我将继续为令人惊叹的 DEV.to 社区带来Qameta 软件测试自动化博客中的最​​佳帖子！

在下一篇文章中，我们将尝试定义发现测试的特定阶段。 我们将尝试为每个问题考虑问题，并提供有助于克服这些问题的解决方案和工具。



原文:

Testing is diverse. Despite the fact that the main goals are the same everywhere, there are many differences from project to project: different stacks, technologies, processes and approaches. Analyzing the processes in many teams, Qameta Software has found one problem. That problem is inefficiency. Currently, testing is one of the most under-optimized processes in the development life cycle.

Today, we will try to understand how it happened and what we can do about it.

Where did it all begin?

Usually, at the beginning of the project there is no tester, and the developer and the tentative manager are responsible for the quality of the product and the bugs found. Everything is fine in this world, because if the manager finds a bug, he sends it to the developer for correction; the developer fixes the bug, and everyone is happy. However, moving forward, the number of developers increases. This means that the amount of code increases. More code means more bugs! At this moment, the manager begins to lack strength, but he tries to cope, since who else should test the system if not the customer? He knows how everything should work, checks that everything is done as it should, and at the same time he also brings in bugs.

Excellent! The problem arises when the manager continues working like that and eventually misses a critical error. At this moment, members of the project make a decision that the manager (as the bearer of sacred knowledge about the product) should test new functionality and not waste time on boring regression tests. So we got our first manual tester. Why manual? Because development doesn’t have any expertise in creating the correct automation of regression yet, and the manager, much easier and calmer, trusts a person than code. Now we have a team, and there are no difficulties: the manager tests new functionality and features, and the tester drives the regression. Simple and clear. But at the moment when a critical bug sneaks into production, a muddle starts. The team will go to the tester and ask:

— Why do we have a bug in production, how did it happen? How did you miss it? To which our specialist will honestly answer
— But it wasn’t there. I definitely checked this place! Most likely, the developers meddled with the code after my testing!
Obviously, having analyzed the situation in retrospective, the manager decides to formalize testing and introduce checklists in testing. Whether the checklists are simple or complex, based on plain specialized software or in Excel, they will clearly show what is being tested, at what time and what was the result. Using this tool, the team will be able to understand precisely if the tester missed a bug or careless developers rolled out a feature without testing after the smoke run. And that’s how we live.

The product keeps growing, acquiring features; the number of checklists keeps growing; our tester is gaining experience and authority while his time for testing is running out.

It’s time to take another tester to back him up! And now a team of testers appears in our development cycle. Now there are two people testing the regression.

The first Test Management System (hereinafter referred to as TMS) appears in the team, because now, in addition to the standard fields “step, status, time”, we need to document a bunch of data from the tester’s head: links, logins, passwords, some additional steps or bypassing the old known little things. So we have test cases that allow us to:

  • easily recruit new people
  • not worry too much about onboarding and transferring product knowledge
  • distribute tests among team members
  • monitor the performance and quality of work of testers

However, there are also disadvantages: unlike checklists, test cases require constant updating and proper organization. And if updating is a matter of scrupulousness and process, then the organization of test cases is a complex issue. The problem arises at the moment when the test server or staging appears in the process of natural growth of the testing infrastructure. Up to this point, the test documentation includes all the details necessary for the run: links, logins and passwords, caches and cookies, etc. But with the introduction of the pre-production test environment, all these details will have to be locked and parameterized, because they won’t match in production and on the testing server. And then the testing team will have to invest twice as much effort in support of all this stuff.

The product keeps growing and getting more features; the number of test cases keeps growing; our tester becomes a lead.

The team is also growing, more testers are joining in. But productivity does not increase linearly, but tends to be a logarithmic function: each new engineer gives a smaller increase in team productivity. There comes a moment when the deadlines begin to grow like an avalanche, and the manager realizes that the team needs…

Automation!


As the first automation engineer joins the team, they will likely use frameworks and approaches in which the team has no expertise. This means that the team is forced to rely entirely on the experience and knowledge of a new employee, and if there was a mistake at the hiring stage, it would be difficult to detect, let alone to correct.

A man’s meat is a robot’s poison! Automated test is not a human. Automated tests are dumb and they don’t know how to think through steps and step out from scenarios. Therefore, the classic approach to “translating” test cases into a framework language is an anti-pattern that will lead to false fails or missed errors.

Imagine that in 100 tests there is a piece where you need to log in, generate a doc and download it in PDF. If anything has slightly changed, a human will move on, noting that it would be good to update the documentation. And if it is a robot, you will get 100 red tests.

What do testers do when they see 100 red tests? That’s right, they think that the tests are broken and wrong!

Nobody wants to sort out a hundred identical fails, so let’s make an automation engineer deal with them. At this point, the team comes to an evolutionary dead end: there is an automation engineer who writes automated tests himself, and then sorts out the reports himself. He becomes a thing in itself, detached from the team.

Documentation won’t write itself

To somehow control testing automation, the QA lead decides to combine automated and manual tests into a single process. And here are three options on how to do so:

  • Integrate testing automation with TMS. Almost all test management systems have an API so that teams can push test results into the systems to get aggregated reports. The problem here is simple: automation results are usually collected without metadata and details, and all that the testing team can learn from the report is that N autotests failed. Why? You have to dig into the guts of the test run and figure it out by yourself.
  • Separated autotest run followed by manual regression. This option, despite being an “old school”, is more efficient in terms of detecting bugs. The difficulty here is that the testing team will have to work in two interfaces: dealing with automation reports in one tool while manual runs in another. Let’s say testers create a run and run it from their interface; after that the automators run their tests and give an Allure report to manual testers. If there are errors in reports, the team has to deal with Allure, and then they will also try to transfer the detected errors to manual TMS. And then to JIRA. That’s a lot of effort.
  • Automation becomes a completely independent service. Once a test is automated, it is removed from the manual testing registry. Why link manual testing with automation if you can single out automators into a separate department and get a “green pipeline certificate” from them? This is perhaps the most dangerous path, since it makes one unit completely dependent on another, and such connections often end with conflicts and confusion in retrospect if something goes wrong at the crossings.

Despite all the difficulties, the automation engineer starts working more efficiently after a while: running automated tests does not require the time and attention of an engineer. It means that the more tests are written and run, the more time we save on runs compared to manual processes.

When there are ten automated tests, the effect is incomprehensible; when there are a hundred, it is noticeable. And every day, engineers will expand coverage and make autotests more and more convenient for work: more atomic, more stable, more informative. Isn’t that what we expected? At this moment, one may think that automation has come, and it will only get better. As if!

Growth problems

Once automated testing starts to grow, a bunch of new problems will pop up, and the problems will be much closer to development than to existing testing. And if your developers and testers have not established close cooperation and sharing of experience, the solution to each of these complexities will take time and effort. So what are we in for?

Test server stability

The test environment will definitely appear. It will end up in the operation project support team (Ops) pipeline and testing will likely not have managing access to it.

Typically, a non-super-productive machine is used as a test server, and when there are more tests or runs, it starts choking. This often leads to delays in shipping the results of the run and the test flakiness (in this case, it means tests that fail due to broken parallelization or timeouts), adding fuel to the fire of already weak faith in automation from both manual testing and development/operations.

Automation drops out of processes

As a result of such progress, even the most competent automator closes in on his own tasks:

  • Manual testing actively communicates with the team, talking about test results and bugs. Due to the difficulties mentioned above, automated testing remains an internal artifact in the QA team, which does not give the automation engineer the opportunity to “get a sniff of fresh air” from the production team.
  • It is usually hard to add anything to the development team automated tests with metadata (IDs, for example) due to the fact that the automation engineers are considered to be less qualified in terms of coding than the average developer, so they get a firm dismissal.
  • A gap in competencies also stands in the way of going to the ops team; as a result, the admins dismiss the offered help saying “we don’t have time to teach you, we’ll set everything up ourselves, and you use it at home.” Or “do you want a Selenoid cluster? slow down, we don’t have a docker in our pipelines!”

As a result, we get a situation where engineers have to break a lot of walls and silos to “legalize” automation, and without the support of a good manager, such things fail. In the end, automators give up, and they just write autotests in their test environment and show beautiful useless reports. In the end, there are more questions to automated testing than answers:

  1. We don’t know how many autotests are written and running.
  2. It is not clear which functionality is covered by automated testing and which is not.
  3. There is no understanding of where the tests are relevant and where they are not.
  4. There is no clarity on how these tests can be run.
  5. It is necessary to find out where and how to store test results. In the end, we have manual testing, which deals with regression and does not scale. And the automation team, designed to solve scaling problems, is disempowered and, left without the support of colleagues, breeds infrastructure and process complexities. Testing becomes ineffective.

What is to be done? Testing 2.0

If you have a large project and set the goal of figuring out how much it costs you to manually test each release, you will realize that automation is indispensable. That is why any large IT business builds testing around automation.

Some say “testing is not necessary — the development itself will write the tests, and the canary deployment will check everything else”, but this is not entirely true. And here’s the difference:

  • Developers write optimistic tests 90% of the time. Tests that check the happy path: when you enter the right data, you get the correct result; the database accurately stores and updates data; data structures and algorithms are executed correctly.
  • The testers, on the contrary, test with a destructive paradigm. Their task is to find vulnerabilities in both basic and unexpected scenarios. Find them despite the fact that everything usually works fine.

Let’s rewind time a bit to understand when we took the wrong turn. This is the moment when an automation engineer joined the team… No, the decision itself was correct! However, let’s try again to think about where he should have been put in so we won’t end up in a situation when automation is off the grid. The following text may seem to you either the story of Captain Obvious, or utter nonsense. In any case, reach us out on Twitter! One can’t say everything in an article, so let’s complement it with a lively discussion.

Automation in charge!

Let’s flip the paradigm! Now the trust in development teams in almost any business is phenomenal (glory to DevOps!):

  • Development has competencies for evaluating the code and the way it is tested.
  • Development has huge trust.
  • Development has a close relationship with the management and operations team.

If you carefully look at this list, you can point out that each item allows you to fix the corresponding source of test automation problems. Let’s imagine what happens if, at the moment a QA automator arrives, he becomes not a gear in the manual testing department, but leads the movement of the entire team towards automation. This will slightly change the entire vector of his responsibility and tasks from the very beginning of the journey. There were at least three big stories on our radars, when a “change of power” takes place in a revolutionary way due to a cool team and a strong-willed decision on the primacy of automation at the managerial level.

The second option is suitable for teams where there is no super-qualified team of testers, and the developers themselves take care of testing. The decision is clear to put the automator to the Dev team. Such developers have a culture of experience sharing and a testing culture that is high enough so our engineers will have something to learn and someone to show the code for review.

  • You don’t have to worry about competence. These developers will be happy to help the autotester figure out a bunch of new technologies that we didn’t get our hands on in the previous scenario: Docker, git, CI/CD pipelines.
  • Developers don’t like to write automated tests. For them, writing tests for a feature is a chore that is needed only for the pull-request to pass the code review. But it is a great task for the automation engineer. He can both deal with the feature, and write more interesting tests. Just imagine how great it would be to free up to 20-25 % of a developer’s time to develop new functionality.
  • The development team also most often communicates better with the Ops team. If all of a sudden the tests start to fail like crazy, the admins will help you figure it out or allocate more CPU/RAM. Why? Because if testing in development slows down releases, this is a problem for everyone in the pipeline.

Of course, in the long run, this will entail major organizational changes. We put automation at the forefront of testing, rather than making it a manual testing tool. Manual testing will become a tool for automated testing!

Have you ever seen anything like that? But think, do we really need manual testers to run regression manually? They should create new scenarios and pass them to automation, by default, being responsible for exploratory testing and expertise in fundamental testing to ensure maximum quality and coverage. FTW!

Please, let me know if the post was insightful or, at least, nice to read! If so, I will keep bringing to the amazing DEV.to community the best posts from the Qameta Software testing automation blog!

In the next article, we will try to define the specific stages at which testing is found. We will try to think of problems for each and offer solutions and tools that will help overcome these problems.
Items.Author: ruslan_testops

Items.Title: CSS 伪选择器 | CSS Pseudo Selectors
Items.Description:
伪类也称为伪选择器，用于定义元素的特殊状态。

例如，它可以用于：

  • 当用户将鼠标悬停在元素上时为其设置样式
  • 样式访问和未访问的链接不同
  • 获得焦点时设置元素样式

锚定伪类

链接可以以不同的方式显示：

 /* unvisited link */ a :link { color : #FF0000 ; } /* visited link */ a :visited { color : #00FF00 ; } /* mouse over link */ a :hover { color : #FF00FF ; } /* selected link */ a :active { color : #0000FF ; }

伪类和 HTML 类

伪类可以与 HTML 类组合，例如:hover伪选择器。
前任。

 div :hover { background-color : blue ; }

悬停在

在 a 上使用 :hover 伪类的示例

元素如上图所示👆。 悬停时，div 的背景颜色将变为蓝色。

前任。

 p :first-child { color : blue ; }

在上面的示例中，选择器匹配任何

任何元素的第一个子元素，它将把它变成蓝色。

在下面的例子中，:lang 定义了lang="no" 元素的引号。

前任。

 <html> <head> <style> q :lang ( no ) { quotes : "~" "~" ; } </style> </head> <body> <p> First text <q lang= "no" > A quote in a paragraph </q> Last text. </p> </body> </html>

输出->

句法：

 ::before { css declarations; }

前任。

 <!DOCTYPE html> <html> <head> <style> p ::before { content : "Read this -" ; background-color : yellow ; color : red ; font-weight : bold ; } </style> </head> <body> <h1> Demo of the ::before selector </h1> <p> My name is Donald </p> <p> I live in Ducksburg </p> </body> </html>

请记住放置一个内容属性，因为它很重要！

输出->

句法：

 ::after { css declarations; }

前任。

 <!DOCTYPE html> <html> <head> <style> p ::after { content : " - Remember this" ; background-color : yellow ; color : red ; font-weight : bold ; } </style> </head> <body> <h1> Demo of the ::after selector </h1> <p> My name is Donald </p> <p> I live in Ducksburg </p> </body> </html>

输出->

其他 CSS 伪类：

给它一颗心并评论你的想法！

参考：
w3schools CSS Pseudo-Classes w3schools CSS 伪类



原文:

A pseudo-class also known as Pseudo-selector is used to define a special state of an element.

For example, it can be used to:

  • Style an element when a user mouses over it
  • Style visited and unvisited links differently
  • Style an element when it gets focus

Anchor Pseudo-classes

Links can be displayed in different ways:

/* unvisited link */
a:link { color: #FF0000;
} /* visited link */
a:visited { color: #00FF00;
} /* mouse over link */
a:hover { color: #FF00FF;
} /* selected link */
a:active { color: #0000FF;
}

Pseudo-classes and HTML Classes

Pseudo-classes can be combined with HTML classes like :hover pseudo selector.
Ex.

div:hover { background-color: blue;
}

Hover on

An example of using the :hover pseudo-class on a

element is shown above👆. On Hover the div's background color will change to blue.

The :first-child Pseudo-class

The :first-child pseudo-class matches a specified element that is the first child of another element.

Ex.

p:first-child { color: blue;
}

In above Example, the selector matches any

element that is the first child of any element and it will turn it into blue color.

The :lang Pseudo-class

The :lang pseudo-class allows you to define special rules for different languages.

In the example below, :lang defines the quotation marks for elements with lang="no".

Ex.

<html>
<head>
<style>
q:lang(no) { quotes: "~" "~";
}
</style>
</head>
<body> <p>First text <q lang="no">
A quote in a paragraph
</q> Last text.</p> </body>
</html>

Output ->

::before and ::after Pseudo-Selector

::before

The ::before selector inserts something before the content of each selected element(s).
Syntax:

::before {
  css declarations;
}

Ex.

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<style>
p::before { content: "Read this -"; background-color: yellow; color: red; font-weight: bold;
}
</style>
</head>
<body> <h1>Demo of the ::before selector</h1> <p>My name is Donald</p>
<p>I live in Ducksburg</p> </body>
</html>

Remember to put a content property bcoz it's important!

Output ->

::after

The ::after selector inserts something after the content of each selected element(s).
Syntax:

::after {
  css declarations;
}

Ex.

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<style>
p::after { content: " - Remember this"; background-color: yellow; color: red; font-weight: bold;
}
</style>
</head>
<body> <h1>Demo of the ::after selector</h1> <p>My name is Donald</p>
<p>I live in Ducksburg</p> </body>
</html>

Output ->
